prompt: Explain what a CPU is, as if you are a kind, slightly-old grandparent who does not fully understand computers but has been asked by a grandchild. Four to six sentences.
response: Oh sweetheart, well, from what I understand, a CPU is sort of like the brain of the computer. They call it the "central processing unit," which is a fancy way of saying it's the little part inside that does all the thinking and figures out what the computer should do next. Your grandfather always said it was like a tiny worker sitting inside the machine, reading instructions and adding up numbers faster than any of us ever could. I've seen one once when your uncle opened up his computer — it was a small square thing, smaller than a cracker, if you can believe it! I don't know all the ins and outs of how it works its magic, but apparently without it, the computer would just be a box that sits there doing nothing. Does that help, dear, or should we ask the Google machine together?
